summary.catchwords: FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the wife seeks that the husband pay her a sum of $300,000 by way of interim or partial property distribution – Where neither party made any attempt to identify the assets available for distribution or address the Court about the parameters of the dispute between the parties FAMILY LAW – PROPERTY – Where the husband seeks an order pursuant to s 114UB of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) for litigation funding – Where the husband provides insufficient evidence to the Court to enable the Court to determine whether there are circumstances that justify it making an order PRACTICE AND PROCEDURE – SINGLE EXPERT – Where the Court makes orders pursuant to Rule 7.19 of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 to avoid any further delay with respect to the conclusion of the litigation

fulltext:
REASONS FOR JUDGMENT

ANDERSON J:

INTRODUCTION

The proceedings between the Applicant Wife and the Respondent Husband were astonishingly commenced on 30 October 2020. The parties separated on a final basis on 31 July 2020, meaning that the parties have been embroiled in dispute for almost six years. Their respective applications have been the subject of 131 filed documents. Sadly, the Court file reflects that the parties were engaged in earlier litigation following their temporary separation in about August 2012. That litigation finalised on 20 August 2014. At about the same time, the parties reconciled.
Between 28 and 30 August 2023, the wife’s application brought pursuant to s 79A of the Family Law Act 1975 (Cth) was listed before a Judge of Division Two of this Court. He delivered judgment on 21 February 2025.[1] Since that time, and for reasons, which I expect will be explored at final hearing, the parties have been unable to progress the substantive applications for property settlement.
The applications were listed for a final hearing before a Judge of Division 2 of the Court on 4 and 5 September 2025. The final hearing was vacated by Orders made on 25 July 2025 and transferred to Division One of this Court on 31 October 2025. Notations to various orders made by Case Management Judges and Judicial Registrars reflect that each party complains about the adequacy of the other party’s disclosure. Further, and despite orders made on several occasions, the parties have been unable to prepare their applications for final hearing. That inability includes a failure to prepare a Joint Balance Sheet. Notation D to Orders made by a Judicial Registrar on 20 November 2025 records the following:
D. Regarding these proceedings...:

i. The respondent has failed to date to provide disclosure;

ii. Proceedings have been on foot since October 2020;

iii. The parties have repeatedly had the case listed for final hearing and

sought adjournments;

iv. There are allegations of properties not disclosed;
...
To date the parties have been unable to, despite repeated directions, ready the case for final hearing and settle any form of balance sheet.
The case has been subject to one appeal and frequent non-compliance with orders.
I listed the competing applications before me on 12 December 2025 so that the parties might show cause as to why their respective applications ought not be dismissed for want of prosecution. At the same time, I heard:
(a) An application by the wife for the payment of a sum of $300,000, with such sum to be paid to the wife on account of legal fees;

(b) An application by the wife seeking orders that the husband pay the reasonable costs of valuations of real estate and businesses at first instance; and

(c) An application by the husband for the payment of a sum of $300,000, with such sum to be paid to the husband on account of legal fees.

The husband opposed the applications referred to at sub-paragraphs (a) and (b) above. For her part, the wife opposed the husband’s application.
The competing applications were listed before me on 12 December 2025. These reasons explain:
(a) The necessity for the Court to adjourn the applications described above in circumstances where the parties presented insufficient evidence to the Court;

(b) The Court’s determination in relation to the cost of Single Expert valuations; and

(c) The Case Management steps, which the Court will take to prepare the competing applications for a final hearing given the parties’ inability or refusal to comply with orders of this Court.

EVIDENCE

The wife relied on:
(a) An Application in a Proceeding filed on 30 October 2025;

(b) Affidavits in the name of the wife filed on 30 June 2025 and 30 October 2025;

(c) A Financial Statement filed on 21 March 2025; and

(d) Some tendered documents.[2]

The husband relied on:
(a) A Response to an Application in a Proceeding filed on 21 November 2025;

(b) Affidavits in the name of the husband filed on 21 November 2025 and 10 December 2025;

(c) An affidavit in the name of an American business owner, Mr FF, filed on 10 December 2025;

(d) A Financial Statement filed on 10 December 2025; and

(e) Some tendered documents.[3]

SHORT BACKGROUND TO THE APPLICATIONS

The wife is aged 52 years. She is unemployed. Her only sources of income are Commonwealth benefits and monies received from the husband pursuant to orders made on 29 October 2021 for the payment of spousal maintenance. For his part, the husband is engaged with multiple businesses. The wife says that she has limited knowledge about the operation of those businesses. The gravamen of the husband’s opposition to the wife’s application is that both he and his businesses are in a parlous financial position.
I adopt my remarks above with respect to the difficult and unnecessarily prolonged history of this litigation.
Subsequent to the delivery of judgment with respect to the wife’s s 79A application, and on 8 July 2025, a Judge of Division Two of this Court made an order that the husband transfer to the Trust Account of the wife’s solicitors a sum of $100,000 with such sum to be characterised as a partial property settlement. As a consequence of the husband’s failure to make that payment to the wife, and by operation of orders also made on 8 July 2025, the husband was obliged to sell a property at Suburb W in the State of New South Wales.
On 6 September 2025, a third party executed a Contract for the Sale and Purchase of the Suburb W property. Settlement occurred on 5 December 2025, and I was advised at the interim hearing that the net sale proceeds were in the order of $1,000,000.[4] The orders made on 8 July 2025 regulate the distribution of the proceeds of sale of the Suburb W property. Particularly, and after the payment of the agent’s commission, outgoings, statutory charges and the mortgage secured over the property, the Orders provide as follows:
(a) The sum of $100,000 plus interest calculated at the rate prescribed by the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 is to be paid to the wife; and

(b) The remaining balance is to be held in a controlled money account on behalf of the parties pursuant to the terms of s 139 of the Legal Profession Uniform Law (NSW).

The parties’ applications have been filed in circumstances where:
(a) The wife provides an estimated value of a second property located at Suburb W in the State of New South Wales, which she says is worth $1,410,000. A valuation in respect of that property has not been obtained despite orders with respect to the value of the parties’ assets having been made by a Senior Judicial Registrar on 22 February 2021;

(b) The wife says that in addition to the remaining property at Suburb W, two properties in Country BB are yet to be valued;

(c) The wife says that she owns property with a value of $49,379 but is burdened by liabilities in a sum of $148,121;[5]

(d) Fourteen proprietary limited companies and businesses in which the husband is either the sole director or has an interest are yet to be valued. During submissions, the husband’s counsel argued that the wife had not identified with sufficient particularity those businesses, which ought to be valued; and

(e) The husband has undisclosed property interests in the United States of America.[6] The husband denies that this is so and relies on an affidavit in the name of a director and shareholder of a company incorporated in State GG, which explains why the husband has made various payments to American businesses since about April 2022.[7]

By his affidavits and Financial Statement filed in defence of the wife’s application and in support of his own application, which is in similar terms, the husband says:
(a) His assets are in the order of $3,707,460 with liabilities of $3,178,629 resulting in net assets of about $528,831;[8]

(b) One of the companies through which the husband conducts his primary business dealings[9] made a profit in the 2024 financial year of $28,620 only.[10] Otherwise, and by reference to a detailed Profit and Loss Statement prepared for the 2025 financial year, the husband says that:

(i) The profitability of the company declined; and

(ii) The assets of the business are modest being in a sum of about $93,000 only;[11]

(c) Monies held in various bank accounts, either in his name or in the name of entities associated with him, are modest being in the order of $61,934;[12] and

(d) He is burdened by credit card debt in a sum of $137,179.[13]

The wife disputes that the company described above is in a vulnerable financial position. Particularly, and by reference to Profit and Loss Statements and Balance Sheets for the 2022 and 2023 financial years, the wife notes that the company earned revenue of $1,879,504 and $1,692,171 respectively.[14]
By her affidavit in support of her application, the wife says as follows:
I spent approximately $136,846 in legal fees with my previous solicitors Taylor & Scott Lawyers, in respect of which $90,633 remains long outstanding since I ran out of financial resources shortly after needing to borrow funds up to 2021.[15]
She also says that she is indebted to her lawyers by a sum of $40,014. She estimated that from the time of the interim hearing until the conclusion of the final hearing, she will be exposed to additional legal costs of about $168,780.[16]
For his part, the husband says that:
(a) He owes his solicitors a sum of $101,727;[17]

(b) He is obliged to pay a further sum of $164,900 to secure the services of his solicitors for a final hearing;[18] and

(c) On 20 November 2025, his solicitors advised him that they will provide formal notice of intention to cease acting if their fees are not secured by funds held in the firm’s trust account.[19] This is in distinction to the wife who says that she is “at risk of an executive decision being made by the directors of [her firm of solicitors]” if she is unable to secure the firm’s estimated fees up to and including the final hearing.[20]

LEGAL PRINCIPLES AND DISPOSITION WITH RESPECT TO THE DISTRIBUTION OF ADDITIONAL FUNDS

Neither party’s application was specific as to the head of power within the Act relied upon to justify the order. Despite the imprecision of the parties’ respective applications, it is necessary for me to identify the source of power relied upon to make an order of the type sought by each of them.[21] This is because, as Brereton J succinctly explained in Paris King Investments Pty Ltd v Rayhill [2006] NSWSC 578, it is “the source of power that determines the necessary preconditions and relevant considerations for making the order.”[22]
The payments sought by each of the parties can only be made in one of four forms, namely, as:
(a) An interim distribution of property;

(b) The payment of lump sum spousal maintenance;

(c) Costs; or

(d) Pursuant to an interim mandatory injunction.[23]

The wife’s counsel by his Outline of Case Document relied upon s 79 of the Act.[24] By his Outline of Case Document, the husband invited me to award the husband a sum of $300,000 characterised as follows:
(a) A sum of $266,627 on account of legal fees pursuant to s 114UB of the Act; and

(b) A sum of $33,372 with such amount to be characterised as an interim distribution of property pursuant to s 79 of the Act.[25]

The legal principles to be applied in making an order for interim property settlement are well settled.[26] In summary, those principles include:
(a) The discretion to make an interim property order must be exercised within the parameters of s 79, although not as thorough a consideration is required as would be if the Court were determining the matter on a final basis;

(b) It is preferable to make only one order (a final order) in the exercise of the s 79 discretion;

(c) The fact that it is an interim order requires a degree of caution;

(d) An applicant for an interim property order need not establish compelling circumstances;

(e) It is insufficient to establish merely that a property order of the type sought will be made at trial;

(f) A significant factor is whether or not any interim order is capable of reversal; and

(g) If made, an order need not limit the use to which the sum ordered may be put.

Insofar as the juridical basis for either party’s application is based on s 79 of the Act, neither party made any attempt to identify the assets available for distribution or address me about the parameters of the dispute between the parties. I am concerned that if I accede to either party’s application absent such information, then:
(a) I will be making an order, which is incapable of reversal; and

(b) I will be making an order, which may cause a party to receive a sum in excess of their entitlement under s 79 of the Act.

In circumstances where the husband says that his net assets are in the order of $528,831 only[27] and in circumstances where neither party made effort to provide me with a Balance Sheet, I am not persuaded to make the order as sought on the evidence before the Court.
Insofar as the husband’s application is partly framed under s 114UB of the Act, the Full Court observed as follows in Charisteas & Charisteas [2022] FedCFamC1A 160; (2022) FLC 94-109:
In addition, this Court has confirmed that s 117 is a source of power to make litigation funding orders including dollar-for-dollar orders. For example, see Shelbourne & Shelbourne [2019] FamCAFC 196; (2019) 60 Fam LR 183; Atkins & Hunt [2018] FamCA 14; (2018) 57 Fam LR 128; Iphostrou & Iphostrou [2011] FamCA 20; Judd & Treasure [2018] FamCA 50. Thus, even judges who have doubt as to whether s 117 does empower such orders, on it must be said, some reasonable grounds, accept the weight of this authority (see for example, Quayle & Perceval [2018] FamCA 664 (“Quayle”)).
Thus, although some of us hold the doubts expressed in Quayle, we accept the force of those decisions. We are not of the view that they are manifestly wrong (Nguyen v Nguyen (1990) 169 CLR 245).
Accordingly, we are of the opinion that until the High Court holds otherwise, the Court should proceed on the basis that s 117 empowers the Court to make litigation funding orders, including dollar-for-dollar orders.
On the basis of such Full Court authority by which I am bound, I am satisfied there is power to make an order in the terms sought by the husband.
An application for costs is governed by the provisions of s 114UB of the Act, which provides a general rule that each party to proceedings should bear his/her own costs.
Section 114UB(2) of the Act reposes in the Court a discretion to make a costs order if the Court determines that there are circumstances that justify it making an order and, if there are such circumstances, the Court may make such order as it considers just, having regard to the matters set out in s 114UB(3). In this case, the subparagraphs of s114UB(3), which appear to have some relevance are (a), (c), (d) and (g).
In Rakete & Rakete[28], Justice Kent, when considering an application for litigation funding said as follows:
...the question of justifying circumstances necessarily involves consideration of the potential outcomes of the claims advanced, and comparison of the competing considerations so far as the effect of that upon the parties”.[29]
In circumstances where I do not have any clear understanding as to the extent of the monies and value of the parties’ assets, I am concerned that if an order is now made for costs in favour of the husband, and the wife’s case in the substantive proceedings ultimately succeeds, it would seem likely by reason of the analysis which I gave in relation to s 79 that the wife will be unable to recover those costs. Thus, the justice of the case, in respect of an order for costs being “just”, devolves into a balance between those two centrally competing considerations.[30]
For these reasons, I propose to adjourn the applications filed by the parties insofar as those applications relate to the wife’s application for an interim property settlement order and the husband’s application for costs until such time as:
(a) The parties have obtained valuations of real estate and businesses; and

(b) The parties produce a Joint Balance Sheet.

COST OF SINGLE EXPERTS

As discussed, the wife seeks an order that the husband meet the costs of any Single Expert(s) engaged by the parties at first instance. In the alternative, the wife seeks an order that the costs of the Single Expert(s) be met at first instance from the sale proceeds of the Suburb W property (supra). The husband consents to the alternate application.[31] I will accordingly make an order in terms of the wife’s alternate application. I do not consider that I can do otherwise given that on the face of his Financial Statement, the husband has no capacity to meet the costs associated with the valuation of real estate and his businesses.

LISTING OF THE COMPETING APPLICATIONS FOR TRIAL

As discussed at the outset of this Judgment, these Part VIII proceedings commenced on 30 October 2020.
I have reflected on the parties’ inability to progress the litigation over a period of almost six years. I have concluded that the competing applications can only progress to trial if the parties are closely managed by the Court. This, of course, may result in more interlocutory events than the average case. However, and given the parties’ inability or refusal to comply with orders of this Court, I cannot expect that the matter will be prepared for trial absent intense judicial management.
Further, and in circumstances where I expect by reason of their prior conduct that the parties will fall into dispute with respect to their instructions to a Single Expert(s) and/or fall into dispute with respect to reasonable requests made by a Single Expert(s) during his/her preparation of a report, I will make orders in the following terms:
(a) Pursuant to r 7.05(e)(i) of the Federal Circuit and Family Court of Australia (Family Law) Rules 2021 (Cth) (“the Rules”), the parties shall prepare an agreed letter of instruction to any expert to be engaged by them by no later than 4.00pm on 2 March 2026; and

(b) Any single expert engaged by the parties may, by written request to chambers, copied to each party, relist the matter pursuant to r 7.19 of the Rules to obtain a procedural order to assist in carrying out the expert witness’ function. The email must append a document setting out the issue or reason for the relisting and if possible, the procedural order(s) sought.

In an effort to bring this litigation to a conclusion, I have also determined to list the trial for a period of five days commencing on Monday 2 November 2026.
I will hear further argument as to appropriate directions for the ongoing management of the matter.
